chapters.title: Sound Waves
chapters.description: Wave motion, properties of sound, reflection, resonance and reverberation

questions.text: Which of the following waves requires a material medium for propagation?
questions.explanation: Sound is a mechanical wave that requires a material medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to propagate. Electromagnetic waves like light, X-rays and gamma rays can travel through vacuum.

questions.text: A tuning fork produces 256 oscillations in 2 seconds. What is its frequency?
questions.explanation: Frequency = Number of oscillations / Time = 256 / 2 = 128 Hz.

questions.text: Which property of a sound wave determines its loudness?
questions.explanation: Loudness is determined by the amplitude of a sound wave. Greater amplitude means greater loudness (intensity).

questions.text: The minimum time gap between the original sound and its echo so that they can be heard separately is:
questions.explanation: The human ear can distinguish between two sounds only if the time gap between them is at least 0.1 second (1/10th of a second). This is the persistence of hearing.

questions.text: Sound travels fastest through which medium?
questions.explanation: Sound travels fastest through solids because the particles are closely packed and the elastic restoring force is greatest. Speed in steel (~5200 m/s) > water (~1500 m/s) > air (~343 m/s).

questions.text: Which phenomenon is responsible for the prolonged sound heard in a large empty hall after the source stops?
questions.explanation: Reverberation is the persistence of sound in a large enclosed space due to repeated reflections from the walls, floor and ceiling after the source has stopped.

questions.text: Transverse waves vibrate in which direction relative to wave propagation?
options: Perpendicular to the direction of propagation
At 45° to the direction of propagation
questions.explanation: In transverse waves, particles vibrate perpendicular (at right angles) to the direction of wave propagation. Example: light waves, water ripples.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of resonance in daily life?
questions.explanation: Soldiers are ordered to break step while crossing a bridge because their regular marching frequency could match the natural frequency of the bridge causing resonance, leading to collapse.

chapters.title: Lenses
chapters.description: Convex and concave lenses, ray diagrams, lens formula and power of a lens

questions.text: A convex lens is also called a:
questions.explanation: A convex lens converges (brings together) parallel rays of light to a focal point, so it is called a converging lens.

questions.text: The focal length of a convex lens is 25 cm. What is its power?
questions.explanation: Power (P) = 1 / focal length (in metres) = 1 / 0.25 m = 4 dioptre (D).

questions.text: When an object is placed at the centre of curvature of a convex lens, the image formed is:
questions.explanation: When the object is at 2F (centre of curvature), the image is also at 2F on the other side — real, inverted, and the same size as the object.

questions.text: A person cannot see distant objects clearly. Which lens is used to correct this defect?
questions.explanation: Myopia (short-sightedness) is corrected using a concave (diverging) lens that shifts the image of distant objects back onto the retina.

questions.text: The lens formula is:
questions.explanation: The lens formula is 1/f = 1/v − 1/u, where f = focal length, v = image distance, u = object distance (all measured from the optical centre).

questions.text: Which device uses a convex lens to produce a magnified, upright, virtual image?
questions.explanation: A simple microscope (magnifying glass) is a single convex lens used to produce a magnified, erect and virtual image when the object is placed within the focal length.

questions.text: Two lenses of power +3 D and −1 D are placed in contact. The resultant power is:
questions.explanation: When lenses are placed in contact, the total power is the algebraic sum: P = P₁ + P₂ = 3 + (−1) = +2 D.

questions.text: The image formed by a concave lens is always:
questions.explanation: A concave (diverging) lens always forms a virtual, upright and diminished image, regardless of object position.

chapters.title: The World of Colours and Vision
chapters.description: Dispersion of light, electromagnetic spectrum, scattering of light, colour vision and defects

test.title: The World of Colours and Vision — Chapter Test

questions.text: The splitting of white light into its component colours by a prism is called:
questions.explanation: Dispersion is the separation of white light into its constituent colours (VIBGYOR) when it passes through a prism, due to different wavelengths bending at different angles.

questions.text: Which colour of light has the highest frequency in the visible spectrum?
questions.explanation: Violet light has the shortest wavelength and therefore the highest frequency among visible colours. Red has the longest wavelength and lowest frequency.

questions.text: Why does the sky appear blue during daytime?
options: Blue light has the highest wavelength
Short wavelength blue light is scattered more by atmospheric particles (Rayleigh scattering)
The sun emits only blue light during day
questions.explanation: Rayleigh scattering causes shorter wavelengths (blue) to scatter much more than longer wavelengths (red) when sunlight passes through the atmosphere, making the sky appear blue.

questions.text: The Tyndall effect is observed when light passes through:
questions.explanation: The Tyndall effect (scattering of light to make the beam visible) occurs when light passes through a colloidal solution whose particles are large enough to scatter light.

questions.text: The primary colours of light are:
questions.explanation: The primary colours of light are Red, Green and Blue (RGB). When all three are combined in equal intensities they produce white light.

questions.text: Why does the sun appear red/orange at sunrise and sunset?
options: The sun produces more red light at those times
At low angles sunlight travels through more atmosphere; blue is scattered away, leaving red
Temperature of the sun is lower at sunrise
Reflection from clouds makes it red
questions.explanation: At sunrise and sunset, sunlight travels through a much thicker layer of atmosphere. Blue and violet light are scattered away, and the longer-wavelength red and orange light reaches our eyes.

questions.text: A person who cannot distinguish between red and green colours suffers from:
questions.explanation: Red-green colour blindness is the most common form of colour blindness, caused by missing or defective cone cells in the retina. It is more common in males and is a sex-linked genetic condition.

questions.text: Which part of the electromagnetic spectrum has wavelength just beyond the red end of visible light?
questions.explanation: Infrared radiation lies just beyond the red end (long wavelength side) of the visible spectrum. Ultraviolet lies beyond the violet end (short wavelength side).

chapters.title: Magnetic Effect of Electric Current
chapters.description: Oersted's experiment, magnetic field due to current, electromagnets, force on a current-carrying conductor, electric motor

test.title: Magnetic Effect of Electric Current — Chapter Test

questions.text: Oersted's experiment demonstrated that:
options: Changing magnetic field produces electric current
Electric current produces a magnetic field
Heat is produced by electric current
Light is produced by magnetic fields
questions.explanation: In 1820, Oersted observed that a compass needle deflects when placed near a current-carrying wire, proving that electric current creates a magnetic field around it.

questions.text: Fleming's Left Hand Rule is used to find the direction of:
options: Force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field
questions.explanation: Fleming's Left Hand Rule gives the direction of force (motion) on a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field. It is used for electric motors.

questions.text: The working principle of an electric motor is based on:
questions.explanation: An electric motor works on the principle that a current-carrying conductor experiences a force when placed in a magnetic field (the motor effect — magnetic effect of current).

questions.text: A soft iron core is used in an electromagnet because:
options: It becomes a strong magnet easily and loses magnetism when current stops
questions.explanation: Soft iron has high permeability (becomes strongly magnetised) and low retentivity (loses magnetism quickly when current is removed), making it ideal for electromagnets.

questions.text: A 100 W bulb and a 40 W bulb are connected in series to the mains. Which bulb glows brighter?
questions.explanation: In series, current is the same. P = I²R. The 40 W bulb has higher resistance (R = V²/P, so lower P means higher R at same voltage). Higher resistance means more power dissipated as heat and light.

questions.text: The device used to detect the presence and direction of electric current is:
questions.explanation: A galvanometer is a sensitive instrument that detects the presence and direction of small electric currents based on the magnetic effect of current.

questions.text: If the current in a wire is reversed, the magnetic field around it:
questions.explanation: The direction of the magnetic field around a current-carrying wire depends on the direction of current flow (Right Hand Thumb Rule). Reversing current reverses the field direction.

questions.text: Which factor does NOT increase the strength of an electromagnet?
options: Increasing the number of turns in the coil
Increasing the resistance of the wire
questions.explanation: The strength of an electromagnet increases with more turns, greater current, and use of a soft iron core. Increasing resistance reduces current, which would weaken the electromagnet.

chapters.title: Electric Energy: Consumption and Conservation
chapters.description: Joule's law, electric power, units of electrical energy (kWh), power transmission and energy conservation

questions.text: The SI unit of electric power is:
questions.explanation: The SI unit of electric power is the watt (W), defined as 1 joule of energy consumed or produced per second. P = V × I.

questions.text: According to Joule's law, the heat produced in a conductor is proportional to:
questions.explanation: Joule's law states: H = I²Rt. Heat produced is proportional to the square of the current, the resistance, and the time for which current flows.

questions.text: 1 kilowatt-hour (kWh) is equal to:
questions.explanation: 1 kWh = 1000 W × 3600 s = 3,600,000 J = 3.6 × 10⁶ J. This is the unit used by electricity meters (1 unit of electricity = 1 kWh).

questions.text: Why is electricity transmitted at very high voltages over long distances?
options: To increase the speed of electricity
To reduce current, which minimises power loss (I²R) in transmission lines
To increase the power at the generating station
questions.explanation: Power loss = I²R. By transmitting at high voltage, the current is reduced for the same power. Since loss ∝ I², reducing I drastically reduces energy wasted as heat in the lines.

questions.text: A 60 W bulb is used for 5 hours daily. How many units (kWh) does it consume in 30 days?
questions.explanation: Energy = Power × Time = 60 W × (5 × 30) h = 60 × 150 = 9000 Wh = 9 kWh = 9 units.

questions.text: A fuse wire in an electric circuit melts when:
options: Current exceeds the safe limit specified for the fuse
questions.explanation: A fuse is a safety device made of a low-melting-point alloy. When current exceeds the fuse's rated value, excessive heat melts it and breaks the circuit, protecting appliances.

questions.text: The purpose of earthing (grounding) in electrical installations is to:
options: Provide a path for current to dissipate safely if a fault occurs
questions.explanation: Earthing connects the metal body of appliances to the ground. If a live wire accidentally touches the metal body, current flows safely to earth preventing electric shock to users.

questions.text: If the transmission voltage is doubled while keeping power constant, the power loss in the line becomes:
questions.explanation: Power loss = I²R. If voltage doubles, current halves (for constant power P = VI). Loss = (I/2)²R = I²R/4. So loss becomes one-quarter of the original.

chapters.title: Electromagnetic Induction in Daily Life
chapters.description: Faraday's law, Lenz's law, AC and DC generators, transformers, mutual induction

questions.text: Electromagnetic induction is the process of:
options: Producing electric current from a changing magnetic field
Converting electrical energy to mechanical energy
questions.explanation: Electromagnetic induction, discovered by Michael Faraday, is the production of an EMF (and thus current) in a conductor when it experiences a changing magnetic field.

questions.text: Lenz's law states that the induced current always acts to:
options: Assist the change that caused it
Oppose the change in magnetic flux that caused it
questions.explanation: Lenz's law states that the direction of induced current is such that it opposes the change in magnetic flux that produced it. This is a consequence of conservation of energy.

questions.text: Fleming's Right Hand Rule is used to find the direction of induced current in a:
questions.explanation: Fleming's Right Hand Rule is the generator rule — it gives the direction of induced current when a conductor moves in a magnetic field, as in a generator.

questions.text: A step-up transformer increases:
questions.explanation: A step-up transformer increases voltage in the secondary coil (more turns) while decreasing current proportionally. Power (P = VI) is conserved (ignoring losses).

questions.text: A transformer has 100 turns in the primary and 500 turns in the secondary. If the primary voltage is 220 V, the secondary voltage is:
questions.explanation: V₂/V₁ = N₂/N₁ → V₂ = 220 × (500/100) = 220 × 5 = 1100 V. This is a step-up transformer.

questions.text: An AC generator produces alternating current because:
options: The coil moves in a straight line
The coil rotates in a magnetic field, reversing the direction of induced current every half turn
questions.explanation: As the coil rotates in the generator, the direction of movement relative to the magnetic field reverses every half rotation, reversing the direction of induced current and producing AC.

questions.text: Why is alternating current (AC) preferred over DC for long-distance power transmission?
options: AC voltage can be stepped up using transformers to reduce current and line losses
AC can be stored in batteries more easily
questions.explanation: Transformers only work with AC. By stepping up AC voltage for transmission (reducing current and thus I²R losses) and stepping it down at the destination, long-distance transmission becomes efficient.

questions.text: The core of a transformer is made of laminated soft iron sheets to:
options: Increase the resistance of the core
questions.explanation: Lamination (thin sheets insulated from each other) breaks up the large eddy current loops that would otherwise form in a solid iron core, reducing energy lost as heat.

chapters.title: Mechanical Advantage in Action
chapters.description: Simple machines, levers, pulleys, inclined planes, mechanical advantage, velocity ratio and efficiency

questions.text: Mechanical advantage of a machine is defined as:
questions.explanation: Mechanical Advantage (MA) = Load / Effort = Output force / Input force. MA > 1 means the machine multiplies the input force.

questions.text: A class 1 lever has its:
questions.explanation: In a Class 1 lever, the fulcrum is between the effort and the load. Examples: seesaw, scissors, crowbar.

questions.text: The efficiency of a machine is always less than 100% because:
options: Some input energy is lost as heat due to friction
Output energy is always greater than input
questions.explanation: Efficiency = (Useful output energy / Total input energy) × 100. In any real machine, friction converts some input energy to heat, so output energy is always less than input energy.

questions.text: A movable pulley gives a velocity ratio of:
questions.explanation: A single movable pulley has a velocity ratio (VR) of 2, meaning the effort moves twice the distance the load moves. This gives a theoretical MA of 2.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of a Class 2 lever?
questions.explanation: In a Class 2 lever, the load is between the fulcrum and the effort. A nutcracker/bottle opener is a classic example. The MA is always greater than 1.

questions.text: The velocity ratio (VR) of a machine is defined as:
questions.explanation: VR = Distance moved by effort / Distance moved by load. It is a fixed value for a given machine, determined only by its geometry, not by friction.

questions.text: If a machine has MA = 4 and VR = 5, its efficiency is:
An inclined plane is used to:
options: Increase the force needed to lift an object
Reduce the effort needed to raise a load by increasing the distance over which it acts
Increase the height an object can reach
questions.explanation: An inclined plane allows a heavy load to be raised with less effort by moving it along a longer slope. Work done is the same (conservation of energy), but effort is reduced.
